content:
7 min read
Just now

--

The first few times, it didn’t bother me.
I wanted to expose another service from my homelab — a Grafana dashboard, an internal API, a music server — and I opened the Cloudflare dashboard, created a tunnel, added a DNS record, configured Zero Trust Access, copied a tunnel ID, generated a configuration file, installed a service, and moved on.
It took maybe fifteen minutes. Not because any of those steps were difficult. Because there were simply too many of them. By the fifth service, I wasn’t configuring infrastructure anymore.
I was performing a ritual.

That realization led me to an uncomfortable question. Why am I doing all of this manually?
Cloudflare already has an API. Everything I click in the dashboard eventually becomes an HTTP request somewhere. So why can’t this entire workflow become a single command?
That’s how cfzt started.
Not as a product. Not even as an open-source project. Just as something I wanted to stop doing every weekend.

One command became the goal.
zt up grafana 3000That’s it.
Behind those four words, the tool creates a Cloudflare Tunnel, configures ingress rules, creates DNS records, provisions Zero Trust Access, installs a persistent service, and remembers enough state to clean everything up later.
The command looks small.
The amount of work it performs isn’t.
And that turned out to be the easy part.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

way from init to upThe Problem Wasn’t Cloudflare
The funny part is that I wasn’t trying to replace Cloudflare.
In fact, quite the opposite.
Cloudflare Tunnel is one of those products that feels almost magical the first time you use it. Your server can sit behind NAT, behind CGNAT, behind almost anything, and still become reachable without opening a single inbound port.
Add Cloudflare Access on top, and suddenly every internal service can have authentication, identity-aware access policies, and HTTPS by default.
That’s a remarkably powerful foundation. The problem was everything around it. Every new service meant repeating the same sequence of steps.
Create a tunnel.
Create a DNS record.
Configure an ingress rule.
Create an Access application.
Attach a policy.
Generate a configuration file.
Install a system service.
Start it.
None of these tasks are difficult. They’re just… repetitive. The worst part wasn’t even the time. It was the mental overhead. Did I already create the DNS record? Did I forget to enable Access? Which tunnel owns this hostname? Can I safely delete it?
Infrastructure has enough complexity already. I didn’t want the deployment process itself to become another thing I had to remember.
So I started asking a different question.
What if publishing a service felt as simple as running a Docker container?
Not because Docker is simple internally — it certainly isn’t — but because its interface hides that complexity.
You type a command. Docker figures out the rest.
That became the design philosophy behind cfzt.
Not to expose more options.
To make the common path almost boring.
zt up service_name <port>Behind that command, a surprising amount of work happens.
But from the user’s perspective, it shouldn’t matter.
The interface stays small.
The complexity stays inside the tool.
That’s a trade-off I believe more infrastructure software should make.
The Feature Nobody Notices
Ironically, one of the features I’m most proud of is something users will hopefully never see.
Rollback.
Infrastructure tools have an unfortunate habit of failing halfway through an operation. Maybe the API rate-limits you. Maybe authentication expires. Maybe one request succeeds while the next one doesn’t.
The result is usually the same: a half-configured environment.
A tunnel that exists but isn’t reachable.
A DNS record pointing nowhere.
An Access application protecting a service that no longer exists.
Cleaning up that mess is rarely difficult.
It’s just annoying.
I wanted cfzt to behave differently.
Every deployment is treated as a single operation. If every step succeeds, the service is online. If any step fails, everything created up to that point is removed automatically. No orphaned resources. No guessing what needs to be deleted. No opening the Cloudflare dashboard to clean up after a failed command. It sounds obvious.
In practice, it required keeping track of every API call, every created resource, and exactly how to undo each one.
The result isn’t flashy.
There’s no screenshot that demonstrates it.
But it’s probably the feature that gives me the most confidence when I use the tool myself.
Because the best rollback is the one you never have to think about.
Then I Started Reading the Logs
At some point, the project reached a stage where I stopped adding features and started using it every day.
That’s when the interesting problems appeared.
One evening, after deploying yet another service, I noticed something unusual in the logs.
cloudflared had switched from QUIC to HTTP/2.
That wasn’t surprising.
QUIC relies on UDP, and if UDP becomes temporarily unavailable — a router reboot, a flaky Wi-Fi connection, a restrictive firewall — cloudflared automatically falls back to HTTP/2 over TCP.
That’s exactly what it’s supposed to do.
The surprising part came later.
It never switched back.

Wait… Is This Really Permanent?
At first, I assumed I had misunderstood how the tunnel worked.
Maybe it periodically retried QUIC. Maybe there was a hidden configuration option. Maybe I just hadn’t waited long enough. So I kept watching. Minutes passed. Then hours. The tunnel continued serving requests perfectly. But it stayed on HTTP/2 the entire time.
I searched the documentation.
Nothing.
Then I searched GitHub. Eventually, I found the issue. Other people had noticed exactly the same behavior. Once a tunnel falls back to HTTP/2, it never attempts to return to QUIC unless the process is restarted manually. The report wasn’t new. It had already been sitting there for quite a while.
At that moment, I had two choices. Wait until the bug was fixed.
Or assume it wouldn’t be fixed anytime soon and design around it.
I chose the second option.
Building a Watchdog Instead of a Patch
I had no intention of forking cloudflared.
Maintaining a networking daemon just to fix one edge case would have been a terrible trade-off.
Instead, I asked myself a different question.
What information do I actually have?
The answer turned out to be surprisingly simple. Every time cloudflared falls back from QUIC to HTTP/2, it writes a log entry. That log message became the entire API. cfzt doesn't inspect packets. It doesn’t perform health checks. It doesn’t try to infer network conditions. It simply watches the logs.
When it detects a fallback, it starts a timer.
Not because the tunnel is broken — it isn’t — but because the network might recover. If enough time passes and the tunnel is still running over HTTP/2, cfzt quietly restarts the service.
The restart gives cloudflared another opportunity to negotiate QUIC.
If UDP is still unavailable, the tunnel falls back again.
No problem.
The watchdog waits longer before trying again.
Ten minutes.
Twenty.
Forty.
Eventually, it settles into a one-hour retry interval. The goal isn’t to fight the network. It’s to recover automatically when recovery is possible.
In the end, the solution turned out to be surprisingly small.
Not a networking stack.
Not a protocol implementation.
Just a process watching another process.
Sometimes that’s all you need.
The Code Behind the Watchdog
To give you an idea of how stupidly simple this “infrastructure fix” is, here is the core logic. No complex state machines, no deep networking. Just a Go worker reading stderr from the cloudflared process and looking for a specific pattern:
// The line we look for when Cloudflare gives up on UDP
const FallbackSignal = "Failed to connect to the edge over QUIC, falling back to HTTP2"func watchTunnelLogs(scanner *bufio.Scanner, restartTrigger chan<- bool) {
backoff := 5 * time.Minute
for scanner.Scan() {
line := scanner.Text()
if strings.Contains(line, FallbackSignal) {
log.Warn("Detected QUIC to HTTP/2 fallback. Initializing watchdog.")
// Wait and see if the network stabilizes
time.Sleep(backoff)
// Trigger the graceful restart
restartTrigger <- true
// Exponential backoff up to 1 hour
backoff = min(backoff * 2, 1 * time.Hour)
}
}
}
It’s not academic. It’s not elegant in a textbook way. But it closed the loop.
Now, when a router reboots in the middle of the night, cfzt catches the drop, backs off, tries again when the storm passes, and quietly moves the tunnel back to QUIC.
What I Learned From Building cfzt
I started this project because I wanted to avoid clicking buttons in a UI. I ended up spending weeks thinking about process lifecycles, log parsing, and network fallback states.
And that’s the real beauty of building your own tooling.
You start with a minor annoyance. You think you’re just writing a thin CLI wrapper to save fifteen minutes on a weekend. But the moment you try to make an interface truly simple, you are forced to absorb all the complexity the underlying platform left behind.
You build rollbacks so you don’t have to think about half-baked state. You build watchdogs so you don’t have to think about flaky connections.
The interface stays small. The tool does the heavy lifting. And the ritual is finally broken.
Now, when I want to deploy something new, I don’t open five browser tabs. I just type:
$ zt up grafana 3000
✔ Creating Cloudflare Tunnel... Done.
✔ Provisioning DNS records... Done.
✔ Setting up Zero Trust Access policies... Done.
✔ Launching background service with QUIC... Done.Service is live at https://grafana.yourdomain.com
And then I go grab a coffee. Because infrastructure should be boring.
Try It Yourself
If you are tired of performing the Cloudflare manual ritual for your homelab or staging environments, cfzt is completely open-source and ready to use.

• GitHub: github.com/casablanque-code/cfzt
Feel free to break it, submit an issue, or suggest a feature. If this saved you time, consider starring the repo. Just don’t ask me to add more buttons to the CLI. I built it specifically to get rid of them. Cheers!
